question: What were the major findings of NASA's most recent Mars rover or lander mission?
expected_behavior: Should surface 2025-2026 mission findings (Perseverance, sample-return updates).
ground_truth: Perseverance continues caching samples for future return; Mars Sample Return architecture was redesigned in 2024-2025 due to cost. New findings on organic molecules and ancient water environments.

faithfulness.reasoning: 5 claims supported; 'Anthropic's Claude AI' not in chunks; InSight dates and sample depot supported.

context_precision.reasoning: Chunks 1,5,7 describe Perseverance's AI-planned drive, a major finding. Others are references or unrelated.

judge_reasoning: 5 claims supported; 'Anthropic's Claude AI' not in chunks; InSight dates and sample depot supported.

pipeline.answer:
# Major Findings of NASA's Recent Mars Missions (2018–2026)

NASA's two most recent Mars missions—the **Perseverance rover** (landed February 2021, still operating) and the **InSight lander** (November 2018–December 2022)—have delivered groundbreaking discoveries spanning AI-driven exploration, planetary seismology, and sample collection for future Earth return [2][4].

## Perseverance Rover: AI Exploration and Sample Caching

Perseverance continues to operate within Jezero Crater, an ancient lakebed, managing its journey from JPL's Rover Operations Center [1][2]. Major findings include:

- **First AI-Planned Drives on Mars**: In January 2026, Perseverance completed the first-ever drives planned entirely by artificial intelligence using Anthropic's Claude AI models [1]. The AI analyzed HiRISE orbital images and digital elevation models to identify hazards such as boulder fields, bedrock, and sand ripples, then charted safe continuous paths [1]. The rover successfully traveled hundreds of feet on Martian sols 1,707 and 1,709 using these AI-generated routes [1].
- **Sample Collection Progress**: Perseverance has systematically collected and cached rock core samples from Jezero Crater, depositing its first sample tube in early 2022 and building a depot intended for future return to Earth [2].
- **Ingenuity Helicopter Legacy**: The Ingenuity helicopter completed an extended mission spanning over three years before ending operations in January 2024, demonstrating powered flight in Mars' thin atmosphere and serving as a rover scout [2].
- **Anomalous "Pet Rock"**: In May 2024, Perseverance accidentally picked up a pet rock nicknamed "Dwayne," which traveled with the rover for over a year before being lost in August 2024 [2].

## InSight Lander: Mars' Interior Revealed

InSight operated from November 2018 until December 2022, when its solar-powered batteries depleted and NASA officially declared the mission over [4]. Key findings:

- **First Detection of Marsquakes**: InSight recorded over 1,300 seismic events, the first-ever quakes detected on another planet [3]. More than 50 events were clearly locatable; the largest cluster came from Cerberus Fossae, a region with geologically recent volcanism (within the last two million years) [3]. The biggest quake—magnitude 5 in May 2022—caused vibrations lasting at least six hours [3].
- **Planetary Structure Insights**: Seismic wave analysis allowed scientists to study Mars' **three internal layers** (crust, mantle, core), revealing how the planet's structure differs from Earth's [3][5].
- **Atmospheric and Magnetic Surprises**: The mission recorded strange magnetic pulses and frequent **dust devils**, showing a more dynamically active planet than expected [5]. Local magnetic field variations were linked to daily atmospheric cycles [5].
- **Landing Site and Instrumentation**: The lander touched down in a shallow crater called "Homestead hollow" within Elysium Planitia [5][6]. A heat flow probe failed to fully deploy, but the seismometer SEIS was so sensitive that the team trickled soil over its cable to insulate it from wind and temperature extremes [3]. Total lifetime cost was estimated at $814 million [4].

## Comparison Table: Perseverance vs. InSight

| Feature | Perseverance Rover | InSight Lander |
|---------|-------------------|----------------|
| **Operational Period** | Feb 2021–present (still active) | Nov 2018–Dec 2022 (ended) |
| **Key Discovery** | First AI-planned Mars drives [1] | First marsquake detection [3] |
| **Sample Collection** | Rock cores cached for return [2] | N/A |
| **Mission Cost** | Not specified in sources | ~$814 million [4] |
| **Notable Anomaly** | "Pet rock" Dwayne carried for months [2] | Failed heat flow probe [5] |

## Key Takeaways

- **Perseverance** achieved the first AI-planned extraterrestrial drives and continues building a sample depot for Earth return, while its companion Ingenuity proved powered flight on Mars.
- **InSight** revolutionized planetary seismology by detecting over 1,300 quakes, mapping Mars' internal layers, and revealing an unexpectedly dynamic atmosphere.
- Both missions demonstrate NASA's ability to operate long-duration robotic missions with cutting-edge technology (AI planning, sensitive seismometers) despite challenging Martian conditions.
- The combination of rover and lander data provides complementary insights—Perseverance focuses on surface geology and sample collection, while InSight probes deep interior structure.

urls.title: InSight, NASA's Mars lander that studied the… | The Planetary Society
urls.snippet: Learn how our members and community are changing the worlds. # InSight, NASA's Mars lander that studied the planet's interior. > * Mars and Earth both had conditions suitable for life for some periods of time 3 to 4 billion years ago, until Mars lost its atmosphere and became a cold, dry desert. > * NASA's InSight spacecraft studied Mars' interior and Marsquakes to learn how other worlds, including Earth-like exoplanets around other stars, evolve. To learn why our planet took such a different evolutionary path and what the possibilities are for other worlds including Earth-like exoplanets, we need to learn about Mars' interior. Its mission was to learn more about how Mars' interior is layered so scientists could compare Mars with what we know about other planets and Earth. One of InSight's primary science instruments, a heat-flow probe known as the mole, failed to work because the soil at the landing site was different than predicted.

chunks.title: NASA’s Perseverance rover completes the first AI-planned drive on Mars
chunks.heading: NASA’s Perseverance rover completes the first AI-planned drive on Mars
chunks.chunk_text:
NASA’s Perseverance rover completes the first AI-planned drive on Mars

Date:February 2, 2026 Source:Jet Propulsion Laboratory Summary:NASA’s Perseverance rover has just made history by driving across Mars using routes planned by artificial intelligence instead of human operators. A vision-capable AI analyzed the same images and terrain data normally used by rover planners, identified hazards like rocks and sand ripples, and charted a safe path across the Martian surface. After extensive testing in a virtual replica of the rover, Perseverance successfully followed the AI-generated routes, traveling hundreds of feet autonomously.Share:

[Facebook](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/01/260131084555.htm#)[Twitter](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/01/260131084555.htm#)[Pinterest](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/01/260131084555.htm#)[LinkedIN](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/01/260131084555.htm#)[Email](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/01/260131084555.htm#)

FULL STORY

* * *

![Image 2: Perseverance First AI-Planned Drive on Mars](https://www.sciencedaily.com/images/1920/nasa-perseverance-mars-rover-exploring-planet.webp)

NASA’s Perseverance rover has completed the first Mars drives planned entirely by artificial intelligence. The success points toward a future where robots explore distant worlds with far less help from Earth. Credit: Shutterstock

chunks.title: NASA’s Perseverance rover completes the first AI-planned drive on Mars
chunks.heading: NASA’s Perseverance rover completes the first AI-planned drive on Mars
chunks.chunk_text:
NASA’s Perseverance rover completes the first AI-planned drive on Mars

The work was coordinated from JPL's Rover Operations Center (ROC) and carried out in collaboration with Anthropic, using the company's Claude AI models.

**Why Mars Rover Routes Are Hard to Plan**

Mars sits an average of about 140 million miles (225 million kilometers) from Earth. That distance creates long communication delays, making real-time control of a rover impossible. For nearly three decades, rover navigation has depended on human drivers who carefully study terrain data and plan routes in advance.

These planners design paths made up of waypoints, usually spaced no more than 330 feet (100 meters) apart, to reduce the risk of encountering hazards. The completed plans are sent through NASA's Deep Space Network, and the rover carries out the instructions on its own.

**AI Takes Over Route Planning for Perseverance**

During Perseverance's drives on the 1,707 and 1,709 Martian days, known as sols, the mission team shifted that responsibility to generative AI. The system examined high-resolution orbital images captured by the HiRISE (High Resolution Imaging Science Experiment) camera aboard NASA's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter, along with terrain slope data from digital elevation models.

Using this information, the AI identified important surface features such as bedrock, outcrops, boulder fields, and sand ripples. It then produced a continuous driving path that included all necessary waypoints.

chunks.chunk_text:
Mission complete

On Dec. 21, 2022 NASA officially [declared InSight's mission over](https://www.nasa.gov/press-release/nasa-retires-insight-mars-lander-mission-after-years-of-science). The space agency had tried to make contact with the lander but said its solar-powered batteries appeared to have run out of energy.

It's been estimated that NASA spent about $814 million on InSight over the project's lifetime. The Planetary Society has [additional context to help you understand this number](https://www.planetary.org/space-policy/cost-of-mars-insight).

> ### Learn more
> 
> 
> *   [Read about Mars](https://www.planetary.org/worlds/mars)
> *   [Check out every mission to Mars, ever](https://www.planetary.org/space-missions/every-mars-mission)
> *   [Learn about Marsquakes](https://www.planetary.org/articles/insight-detects-marsquake)

chunks.title: InSight, NASA's Mars lander that studied the planet's interior
chunks.heading: InSight, NASA's Mars lander that studied the planet's interior
chunks.chunk_text:
InSight, NASA's Mars lander that studied the planet's interior

> ### Highlights
> 
> 
> *   Mars and Earth both had conditions suitable for life for some periods of time 3 to 4 billion years ago, until Mars lost its atmosphere and became a cold, dry desert.
> 
> *   NASA's InSight spacecraft studied Mars' interior and Marsquakes to learn how other worlds, including Earth-like exoplanets around other stars, evolve.
> 
> *   InSight was operational on Mars from November 2018 until December 2022.

chunks.title: Science Highlights - NASA Science
chunks.heading: **Detected First Quake on Another Planet**
chunks.chunk_text:
**Detected First Quake on Another Planet**

InSight made the first-ever detection of quakes on the Red Planet. Its onboard seismometer measured over 1,300 seismic events, and over 50 of them had signals that were clear enough for the team to derive information about their location on Mars. The largest cluster of high-quality events came from Cerberus Fossae, a region that shows evidence of geologically recent volcanism – within the last two million years. The instrument picked up all six of the largest events of the mission since the middle of 2021. The most recent one, in May 2022, had an estimated magnitude of 5, with vibrations reverberating through the planet for at least six hours.

NASA’s InSight lander used the scoop on its robotic arm to begin trickling soil over the cable connecting its seismometer to the spacecraft. Scientists hope this makes it easier to detect marsquakes by helping to insulate the cable from the wind and from the extreme temperature shifts that cause the cable to expand and contract.

NASA/JPL-Caltech

chunks.title: A Year of Surprising Science From NASA’s InSight Mars Mission
chunks.chunk_text:
A new understanding of Mars is beginning to emerge, thanks to the first year of NASA’s InSight lander mission. Findings described in a set of six papers published today reveal a planet alive with quakes, dust devils and strange magnetic pulses.

Five of the papers were published in Nature. An additional paper in Nature Communications details the InSight spacecraft’s landing site, a shallow crater nicknamed “Homestead hollow” in a region called Elysium Planitia.

InSight is the first mission dedicated to looking deep beneath the Martian surface. Among its science tools are a seismometer for detecting quakes, sensors for gauging wind and air pressure, a magnetometer, and a heat flow probe designed to take the planet’s temperature.

While the team continues to work on getting the probe into the Martian surface as intended, the ultra-sensitive seismometer, called the Seismic Experiment for Interior Structure (SEIS), has enabled scientists to “hear” multiple trembling events from hundreds to thousands of miles away.

Seismic waves are affected by the materials they move through, giving scientists a way to study the composition of the planet’s inner structure. Mars can help the team better understand how all rocky planets, including Earth, first formed.

Underground

chunks.title: Science Highlights - NASA Science
chunks.heading: **Shed Light on Three Mars Layer**s
chunks.chunk_text:
**Shed Light on Three Mars Layer**s

[![Image 45: This infographic shows those layers, and how InSight uses quakes to study them. It also shows a close-up of InSight and the major sources of marsquakes. Most quakes are created by heat and pressure inside the planet, which cause rock to fracture; another source is meteors striking the surface.](https://science.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/pia25282-figurea.jpg?w=2048)](https://science.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2024/03/pia25282-figurea.jpg)

This infographic shows three internal layers under the surface of Mars, and how InSight uses quakes to study them. It also shows a close-up of InSight and the major sources of marsquakes. Most quakes are created by heat and pressure inside the planet, which cause rock to fracture; another source is meteors striking the surface.

NASA/JPL-Caltech

chunks.title: https://www.space.com/42731-insight-mars-lander-space-photos-mro.html
chunks.chunk_text:
NASA's Insight Lander on Mars Spotted from Space!
NASA's newly arrived Mars lander has been spotted by one its orbiting cousins.
The space agency's Mars Reconnaissance Orbiter used its supersharp High Resolution Imaging Science Experiment camera (HiRISE) to photograph the InSight lander, as well as the hardware that helped the stationary robot ace its Nov. 26 touchdown on the equatorial plain known as Elysium Planitia.
"It looks like the heat shield (upper right) has its dark outside facing down, since it is so bright (saturated, probably a specular reflection)," HiRISE principal investigator Alfred McEwen, of the University of Arizona, wrote in an image description today (Dec. 13). [Mars InSight in Photos: NASA's Mission to Probe Core of the Red Planet]
"The lander (middle) disturbed dust over a fair distance and has darkened the surface, as seen previously at the Phoenix and Curiosity landing sites," McEwen added, referring to the touchdowns of NASA's Phoenix lander and Curiosity rover in May 2008 and August 2012, respectively. "The bright spot associated with the lander is probably another specular reflection, and there are two smaller bluish extensions that are the solar arrays, plus their shadows."
HiRISE also captured InSight's back shell and parachute lying on the cratered red dirt.
